subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, and welcome to your very first science unit of Year 3! In this unit you are going to become a real scientist. A scientist is simply a person who looks carefully at the world, asks good questions, and tries to find true answers. You already do this every day without even knowing it. When you wonder why the camel by your house chews all day, or why the acacia tree grows new green leaves after the rains come, you are thinking like a scientist.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Sort things into three groups: living, dead, and never-alive — and explain why.
Name and describe the seven life processes that every living thing does (we call them MRS GREN).
Explain how a plant makes its own food and gives off the oxygen that you and every animal breathe.
Describe how a seed wakes up and grows into a new plant — a process called germination.
Give reasons using science words, not just guesses, for every answer you make.
Plan and do a safe, careful investigation at home using things from your own kitchen.
Connect what plants and animals need to what you and your own family need every day.

concepts.title: The Three Groups: Living, Dead, and Never-Alive
concepts.explanation: Let us begin our science adventure with a walk. Imagine you step outside your home in the early morning, when the air is cool and the sky is turning gold. What do you see? Maybe a goat chewing quietly. Maybe an acacia tree with long thorns. Maybe a stone by the path, a metal gate, a puddle of water, and a dried brown leaf that fell in the night. All of these things are around you — but they are not the same kind of thing. Some are alive, some used to be alive, and some were never alive at all. A living thing is…
concepts.example: A dead thing was alive before, but it has stopped. That dried brown leaf on the path was once a green living leaf, joined to a tree, drinking water and catching sunlight. Now it has fallen, dried up, and stopped doing…

concepts.title: The Seven Life Processes (MRS GREN)
concepts.explanation: How do scientists know for sure whether something is alive? They do not just guess. They check a special list of seven jobs that every single living thing must do. Every plant, every animal, every person, and even the tiniest creature too small to see does all seven of these jobs. If something does all seven, it is alive. Scientists call these the seven life processes. Seven things are a lot to remember, so scientists made a clever nickname to help. Take the first letter of each process and you can spell out the…
concepts.example: Living things move some part of themselves.

concepts.title: How Plants Make Food and Give Us Air to Breathe
concepts.explanation: Now we come to one of the most wonderful secrets in all of science. You need food to live, and you get it by eating. But a plant never eats a single meal — and yet it grows from a tiny seed into a giant baobab tree. How can this be? The answer is that a plant is a food factory. It makes its own food from things it gathers around itself. This process is called photosynthesis, which is a long word that simply means "making things with light." To make its food, a green plant needs three ingredients, like a cook needs…
concepts.example: Inside the leaf, something amazing happens. The plant uses the energy from sunlight to mix the water and carbon dioxide together and turn them into sugar, which is the plant's food. This sugar gives the plant energy to…

concepts.title: Seeds and Germination: New Life Begins
concepts.explanation: Have you ever soaked beans overnight to cook them, and noticed that after a day or two they start to sprout a tiny white tail? That tiny tail is the beginning of a brand new plant! The waking up of a seed and the start of its growth is called germination. It is one of the clearest ways to see the life process of reproduction and growth happening right in front of your eyes. A seed may look small, hard, and lifeless, like a little stone. But do not be fooled — inside every seed is a tiny sleeping baby plant, packed…
concepts.example: What does a seed need to wake up and germinate? It needs water to soften its hard coat and wake the baby plant inside. It needs warmth, which is why seeds sprout well in the warm East African soil. And it needs air,…

explorations.title: Wake Up a Seed (Germination)
explorations.context: To watch a seed germinate and discover what a seed needs to wake up and start growing.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you think will happen. Which seeds will grow — the ones with water, or the ones without? How many days until you see a root? A hypothesis is your clever guess before you begin.
explorations.answer: Which jar's seeds germinated? Which part of the seed appeared first? Why did the dry seeds in Jar B not grow? What were they missing? Which life processes from MRS GREN did you see the growing seeds show?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To watch a seed germinate and discover what a seed needs to wake up and start growing.

explorations.title: Does a Plant Follow the Light?
explorations.context: To find out if a plant can move and respond to the direction of light (a life process called sensitivity).
explorations.prompt: Which way do you think the plant will grow — straight up, or toward the window? Write your guess before you begin.
explorations.answer: Which direction did the plant lean at first? Why do you think it leaned that way? After you turned the pot, what did the plant do over the next two days? Why is it helpful for a plant to grow toward the light? (Think about photosynthesis.)
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out if a plant can move and respond to the direction of light (a life process called sensitivity).

explorations.title: The Classification Detective Hunt
explorations.context: To practise sorting real objects into living, dead, and never-alive, and to explain your reasoning like a scientist.
explorations.prompt: Which do you think will be easiest to sort — the living things, the dead things, or the never-alive things? Which might be tricky?
explorations.answer: Which objects were the hardest to place? Why were they tricky? Did any objects fool you at first? For example, did you nearly call the wooden spoon never-alive? Choose one dead object and explain how you knew it was dead and not never-alive.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To practise sorting real objects into living, dead, and never-alive, and to explain your reasoning like a scientist.

explorations.title: Do Plants Breathe Out? (Bubbles of Life)
explorations.context: To look for signs that a green water plant gives off a gas (oxygen) when it sits in the sunlight.
explorations.prompt: Do you think you will see more bubbles in bright sunlight or in a dark corner? Write your guess.
explorations.answer: Where did you see more bubbles — in the light or in the dark? Why? The bubbles are a gas the plant gives off. Which gas do you think it is, and which living things need it? How does this experiment connect to the food-making process called photosynthesis?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To look for signs that a green water plant gives off a gas (oxygen) when it sits in the sunlight.

methods.title: Wake Up a Seed (Germination)
methods.example: To watch a seed germinate and discover what a seed needs to wake up and start growing.
steps: Put a thick layer of damp cotton wool or paper towel inside each jar.
Place 4 or 5 beans between the cotton and the glass, where you can see them clearly.
Label the jars: Jar A gets water, Jar B stays dry.
For Jar A, keep the cotton damp each day (not flooded). Give Jar B no water at all.
Put both jars in a warm place with some light. Check them at the same time every day for one week.

methods.title: Does a Plant Follow the Light?
methods.example: To find out if a plant can move and respond to the direction of light (a life process called sensitivity).
steps: Place your potted plant on a windowsill where light comes from one side only.
Put a mark on the side of the pot facing the window so you remember its position.
Look at which way the leaves and stem are pointing and write it down.
Leave the plant for three days, keeping the soil lightly damp.
On day four, look again. Then turn the pot halfway around and watch over the next two days.

methods.title: The Classification Detective Hunt
methods.example: To practise sorting real objects into living, dead, and never-alive, and to explain your reasoning like a scientist.
steps: Collect your ten objects and place them all together in the middle of a table.
Pick up each object one at a time and look at it closely.
For each object, ask the two detective questions: Is it doing life processes now? Did it ever live or come from something living?
Place the object on the correct sheet: Living, Dead, or Never-alive.
In your notebook, write one sentence of reasoning for each object.

methods.title: Do Plants Breathe Out? (Bubbles of Life)
methods.example: To look for signs that a green water plant gives off a gas (oxygen) when it sits in the sunlight.
steps: Fill the clear bowl with room-temperature water.
Push the fresh green stems down so the leaves are fully underwater.
Place the bowl on a bright, sunny windowsill.
Watch closely for 20 to 30 minutes. Look for tiny bubbles forming on the leaves.
Then move the bowl to a dark corner or cover it with a box, and compare the number of bubbles.

workedExamples.title: Being a Classification Detective
workedExamples.prompt: When scientists sort things, they follow careful steps instead of guessing. Let us solve one puzzle together, step by step, so you can see how a real scientist thinks. Our mystery object is a wooden stool in the corner of the room.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 — Look closely. The stool is made of hard, brown wood. It has grain lines running through it. It does not move on its own and feels dry. Step 2 — Ask: is it doing the life processes now? Is the stool growing? No. Is it eating, breathing, or making baby stools? No. So it is not living right now. Step 3 — Ask: was it ever alive, or did it come from something alive? Yes! Wood comes from a tree, and a tree is a living thing. The tree was cut down and shaped into a stool. Step 4 — Decide. Because the stool came…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a living thing? a) a rock b) a camel c) a metal spoon d) a plastic bucket
workedExamples.solution: b) a camel. A camel does all seven life processes, so it is living. The others never lived.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which word list names the seven life processes? a) big, small, hot, cold, wet, dry, hard b) movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition c) red, blue, green, tall, short, round, flat d) happy, sad, tired, hungry, cold, warm, sleepy
workedExamples.solution: b) movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition. These are the seven life processes, remembered as MRS GREN.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: A dried acacia leaf on the ground is best described as: a) living b) dead c) never-alive d) a gas
workedExamples.solution: b) dead. The leaf was once alive on a tree but has stopped doing life processes, so it is dead — not never-alive.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is never-alive? a) a goat b) a mango tree c) a river stone d) a bean seed
workedExamples.solution: c) a river stone. A stone has never been alive. A goat, a tree, and a seed are all living.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Plants make their own food using sunlight, water, and: a) oxygen b) carbon dioxide c) soil only d) milk
workedExamples.solution: b) carbon dioxide. Plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide from the air to make food.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: What gas do plants give off that animals need to breathe? a) carbon dioxide b) smoke c) oxygen d) steam
workedExamples.solution: c) oxygen. Plants give off oxygen, the gas animals and people breathe in to live.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: What is it called when a seed wakes up and starts to grow? a) reproduction b) germination c) excretion d) respiration
workedExamples.solution: b) germination. Germination is when a resting seed wakes up and begins to grow.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a living thing? a) a rock b) a camel c) a metal spoon d) a plastic bucket
practice.answer: b) a camel. A camel does all seven life processes, so it is living. The others never lived.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which word list names the seven life processes? a) big, small, hot, cold, wet, dry, hard b) movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition c) red, blue, green, tall, short, round, flat d) happy, sad, tired, hungry, cold, warm, sleepy
practice.answer: b) movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition. These are the seven life processes, remembered as MRS GREN.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A dried acacia leaf on the ground is best described as: a) living b) dead c) never-alive d) a gas
practice.answer: b) dead. The leaf was once alive on a tree but has stopped doing life processes, so it is dead — not never-alive.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is never-alive? a) a goat b) a mango tree c) a river stone d) a bean seed
practice.answer: c) a river stone. A stone has never been alive. A goat, a tree, and a seed are all living.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Plants make their own food using sunlight, water, and: a) oxygen b) carbon dioxide c) soil only d) milk
practice.answer: b) carbon dioxide. Plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide from the air to make food.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What gas do plants give off that animals need to breathe? a) carbon dioxide b) smoke c) oxygen d) steam
practice.answer: c) oxygen. Plants give off oxygen, the gas animals and people breathe in to live.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What is it called when a seed wakes up and starts to grow? a) reproduction b) germination c) excretion d) respiration
practice.answer: b) germination. Germination is when a resting seed wakes up and begins to grow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: How does a plant show movement? a) it walks b) it runs away c) it grows toward the light d) it swims
practice.answer: c) it grows toward the light. Plants move slowly by growing, especially toward light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these was once alive but is now dead? a) a glass jar b) a wooden stool c) a metal gate d) a stone
practice.answer: b) a wooden stool. Wood comes from a once-living tree, so the stool is dead. The others were never alive.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which two words describe getting rid of waste, and making new living things? a) excretion and reproduction b) growth and movement c) nutrition and respiration d) sensitivity and oxygen
practice.answer: a) excretion and reproduction. Excretion is removing waste; reproduction is making new living things.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: MRS GREN is a way to remember: a) the colours of plants b) the seven life processes c) the names of animals d) the days of the week
practice.answer: b) the seven life processes. MRS GREN is a memory helper for the seven processes.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A goat eating grass is showing which life process? a) reproduction b) nutrition c) excretion d) movement only
practice.answer: b) nutrition. Eating to get food and energy is the life process called nutrition.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Wake Up a Seed (Germination)
activities.materials: A handful of dried beans, lentils, or mung beans from the kitchen.; Two clear glass jars or clean plastic cups.; Cotton wool, or a folded paper towel, or a clean cloth.; Water.
steps: Put a thick layer of damp cotton wool or paper towel inside each jar.
Place 4 or 5 beans between the cotton and the glass, where you can see them clearly.
Label the jars: Jar A gets water, Jar B stays dry.
For Jar A, keep the cotton damp each day (not flooded). Give Jar B no water at all.
Put both jars in a warm place with some light. Check them at the same time every day for one week.
Safety: Glass breaks — carry jars and mirrors with two hands, and tell an adult straight away if anything chips or cracks.

activities.title: Does a Plant Follow the Light?
activities.materials: A small potted plant, or a bean seedling you grew in Experiment 1 planted in a cup of soil.; A sunny window ledge.; A small sticker or piece of tape to mark the pot.
steps: Place your potted plant on a windowsill where light comes from one side only.
Put a mark on the side of the pot facing the window so you remember its position.
Look at which way the leaves and stem are pointing and write it down.
Leave the plant for three days, keeping the soil lightly damp.
On day four, look again. Then turn the pot halfway around and watch over the next two days.

activities.title: The Classification Detective Hunt
activities.materials: Ten safe objects from around your home (for example: a houseplant leaf, a dried leaf, a wooden spoon, a metal spoon, a stone, a plastic cup, a piece of cloth, a fresh onion, a glass jar, a date).; Three sheets of paper labelled Living, Dead, and Never-alive.; Your science notebook and a pencil.
steps: Collect your ten objects and place them all together in the middle of a table.
Pick up each object one at a time and look at it closely.
For each object, ask the two detective questions: Is it doing life processes now? Did it ever live or come from something living?
Place the object on the correct sheet: Living, Dead, or Never-alive.
In your notebook, write one sentence of reasoning for each object.
Safety: Glass breaks — carry jars and mirrors with two hands, and tell an adult straight away if anything chips or cracks.

activities.title: Do Plants Breathe Out? (Bubbles of Life)
activities.materials: A clear glass bowl or large jar.; A few fresh green leafy stems (pondweed if you have any, or fresh leafy herbs like mint).; Water at room temperature.; A sunny windowsill.
steps: Fill the clear bowl with room-temperature water.
Push the fresh green stems down so the leaves are fully underwater.
Place the bowl on a bright, sunny windowsill.
Watch closely for 20 to 30 minutes. Look for tiny bubbles forming on the leaves.
Then move the bowl to a dark corner or cover it with a box, and compare the number of bubbles.

activities.title: Observe: The Three Groups: Living, Dead, and Never-Alive
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the three groups: living, dead, and never-alive around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: The Seven Life Processes (MRS GREN)
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the seven life processes (mrs gren) around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: [Key] Remember! Every living thing does all SEVEN life processes: M - Movement R - Respiration S - Sensitivity G - Growth R - Reproduction E - Excretion N - Nutrition If something does all seven, it is ALIVE.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: [Sort] Sorting Made Simple LIVING: doing the life processes right now (a camel, a tree, you). DEAD: was once alive, or came from something alive, but has stopped (a dried leaf, wood, leather, meat). NEVER-ALIVE: never lived at all (rock, metal, plastic, glass, water). Key question: Did it ever live? If yes but not now, it is DEAD. If never, it is NEVER-ALIVE.

rules.title: Key idea 4
rules.text: [Recipe] The Recipe Water + Carbon dioxide + Sunlight -> Sugar (plant food) + Oxygen (for us to breathe). Roots drink water. Leaves take in carbon dioxide and catch sunlight. The plant makes its own food and gives us the oxygen we breathe.

terms: Alive right now and doing all seven life processes.
Was once alive but has stopped the life processes.
Has never been alive at any time.
Getting or making food for energy.
Releasing energy from food using oxygen.
How a plant makes food from sunlight, water, and air.
The gas living things breathe in; plants make it.
The gas animals breathe out and plants take in.
When a seed wakes up and starts to grow.
Sensing and responding to the world.
Making new living things of the same kind.

vocabulary.meaning: Alive right now and doing all seven life processes.
vocabulary.example: Some things are living — they are alive right now, like you, like a mango tree, like a flamingo standing in the Rift Valley lakes.

vocabulary.meaning: Was once alive but has stopped the life processes.
vocabulary.example: Some things are dead — they were once alive but are not alive any more, like a dried leaf, a wooden spoon, or a leather sandal.

vocabulary.meaning: Has never been alive at any time.
vocabulary.example: And some things are never-alive — they have never, ever been alive, like a stone, a metal cooking pot, or a plastic bucket.

vocabulary.meaning: Getting or making food for energy.
vocabulary.example: So both plants and animals have nutrition, but plants are the clever cooks who make their own meals.

vocabulary.meaning: Releasing energy from food using oxygen.
vocabulary.example: Respiration is a big word for a hidden job that happens inside every living thing, all day and all night.

vocabulary.meaning: How a plant makes food from sunlight, water, and air.
vocabulary.example: This amazing plant power is called photosynthesis, and we will explore it closely in the next part.

vocabulary.meaning: The gas living things breathe in; plants make it.
vocabulary.example: Explain how a plant makes its own food and gives off the oxygen that you and every animal breathe.

vocabulary.meaning: The gas animals breathe out and plants take in.
vocabulary.example: Second, it needs a gas from the air called carbon dioxide, which it takes in through tiny holes in its leaves.

vocabulary.meaning: When a seed wakes up and starts to grow.
vocabulary.example: Describe how a seed wakes up and grows into a new plant — a process called germination.

vocabulary.meaning: Sensing and responding to the world.
vocabulary.example: Sensitivity means noticing changes around you and responding to them.

vocabulary.meaning: Making new living things of the same kind.
vocabulary.example: Reproduction means making new living things of the same kind.
Getting rid of this waste is called excretion.

commonMistakes: Rivers, cars, and clouds move but are not alive.
Plants do all seven life processes.
Dead things once lived; never-alive things never did.
A seed is alive but resting.
Breathing moves air; respiration releases energy inside.

connections.text: your lungs, heart, and stomach help you do the same life processes you learned here — breathing, respiration, and nutrition.
many never-alive things are sorted by what they are made of, such as wood, metal, plastic, and glass.
the savanna, coast, and river habitats around you are homes full of living things.
caring for plants and animals is part of being a good caretaker (khalifah) of Allah's creation.
Grow different seeds (beans, lentils, cress) and compare how fast each germinates.
Watch a plant near your home over a week and note which life processes you can spot.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: The toy car is not alive because it cannot grow bigger by itself and cannot reproduce (make baby cars). It also cannot feed itself or sense the world. Moving alone is not enough — a living thing must do all seven processes.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The toy car is not alive because it cannot grow bigger by itself and cannot reproduce (make baby cars). It also cannot feed itself or sense the world. Moving alone is not enough — a living thing must do all seven processes.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan says his toy car is alive because it moves and needs fuel. Using MRS GREN, give two reasons why the car is NOT alive.
realProblems.answer: Camel milk is dead (or once-living). It came from a living camel but the milk itself is not doing life processes. Accept 'once alive / not alive now' with good reasoning.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Camel milk is dead (or once-living). It came from a living camel but the milk itself is not doing life processes. Accept 'once alive / not alive now' with good reasoning.

realProblems.prompt: Khadija drinks fresh camel milk. Is the milk living, dead, or never-alive? Explain your thinking.
realProblems.answer: The seeds were resting and alive in the dry soil. When the rains bring water and the soil is warm, the seeds germinate and grow, so the land turns green quickly.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The seeds were resting and alive in the dry soil. When the rains bring water and the soil is warm, the seeds germinate and grow, so the land turns green quickly.

realProblems.prompt: During the dry season the land is brown, but after the rains it turns green quickly. Explain what happens to the seeds in the soil.
realProblems.answer: Families should plant and protect trees because trees make oxygen for everyone to breathe and clean the air. More trees means more oxygen and cooler shade.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Families should plant and protect trees because trees make oxygen for everyone to breathe and clean the air. More trees means more oxygen and cooler shade.

realProblems.prompt: Why should families in a town plant and protect trees? Give a reason using what you learned about oxygen.
realProblems.answer: The seashell is dead (once-living). It was made by a living sea animal but the shell itself is no longer alive. It is not never-alive because it came from a living creature.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The seashell is dead (once-living). It was made by a living sea animal but the shell itself is no longer alive. It is not never-alive because it came from a living creature.

realProblems.prompt: Omar found a white seashell on the beach at Lamu. Is it living, dead, or never-alive? Explain carefully.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What are the three groups we can sort everything into?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The three groups are living, dead, and never-alive.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What does MRS GREN stand for? List all seven.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition. These are the seven life processes.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between a dead thing and a never-alive thing. Give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A dead thing was once alive but has stopped (example: a dried leaf). A never-alive thing has never lived (example: a stone). The key difference is whether it ever lived.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three things a green plant needs to make its own food.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A green plant needs sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to make its food.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Where does the oxygen you breathe come from?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The oxygen we breathe comes from plants, which give it off when they make food by photosynthesis.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What three things does a seed need in order to germinate?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A seed needs water, warmth, and air to germinate. (It does not need light to start.)

questions.question: Which of these is a living thing?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a camel. A camel does all seven life processes, so it is living. The others never lived.

questions.question: Which word list names the seven life processes?
options: big, small, hot, cold, wet, dry, hard
movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition
red, blue, green, tall, short, round, flat
happy, sad, tired, hungry, cold, warm, sleepy
questions.answer: movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, nutrition. These are the seven life processes, remembered as MRS GREN.

questions.question: A dried acacia leaf on the ground is best described as:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: dead. The leaf was once alive on a tree but has stopped doing life processes, so it is dead — not never-alive.

questions.question: Which of these is never-alive?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a river stone. A stone has never been alive. A goat, a tree, and a seed are all living.

questions.question: Plants make their own food using sunlight, water, and:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: carbon dioxide. Plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide from the air to make food.

questions.question: What gas do plants give off that animals need to breathe?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: oxygen. Plants give off oxygen, the gas animals and people breathe in to live.

questions.question: What is it called when a seed wakes up and starts to grow?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: germination. Germination is when a resting seed wakes up and begins to grow.

questions.question: How does a plant show movement?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: it grows toward the light. Plants move slowly by growing, especially toward light.

questions.question: Which of these was once alive but is now dead?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a wooden stool. Wood comes from a once-living tree, so the stool is dead. The others were never alive.

questions.question: Which two words describe getting rid of waste, and making new living things?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: excretion and reproduction. Excretion is removing waste; reproduction is making new living things.

questions.question: MRS GREN is a way to remember:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the seven life processes. MRS GREN is a memory helper for the seven processes.

questions.question: A goat eating grass is showing which life process?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: nutrition. Eating to get food and energy is the life process called nutrition.

selfAssessment: I can sort things into three groups: living, dead, and never-alive — and explain why.
I can name and describe the seven life processes that every living thing does (we call them MRS GREN).
I can explain how a plant makes its own food and gives off the oxygen that you and every animal breathe.
I can describe how a seed wakes up and grows into a new plant — a process called germination.
I can give reasons using science words, not just guesses, for every answer you make.
I can plan and do a safe, careful investigation at home using things from your own kitchen.
I can connect what plants and animals need to what you and your own family need every day.
